title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Re-anchoring: how did we SEE 2026-XJ in the first place?
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — The data came from waves

Every number we've used about the asteroid — its position, its velocity, its mass — was derived from BOUNCED RADIO WAVES. Unit 6 is the framework that made that possible.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Waves carry energy + information without carrying matter. R4. Why radar works (preview to Day 15).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Where we are: end of Unit 5. We've computed impact damage including thermal radiation. But every NUMBER we've used about 2026-XJ — distance, velocity, mass estimate — came from somewhere.

Today's pivot: NASA TRACKED 2026-XJ with radar. Radio waves were sent OUT from Earth, bounced off the asteroid, and returned. The TIME tells distance. The FREQUENCY SHIFT tells velocity. Unit 6 explains every step.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Return Unit 5 transfer tasks. Highlight strong atmospheric-entry reasoning. "Today: where did all the asteroid data COME from? Radar. Today we start understanding radar."
5–18 min

• Demo: a slinky stretched along the floor. Two students hold ends. Pulse it side-to-side (transverse). Then push-pull (longitudinal). Then both at once. Ask: what TRAVELED down the slinky? (Energy. Information. Not the coils themselves.)
18–28 min

• Notice & Wonder. Push toward the fundamental insight: waves carry energy and information WITHOUT carrying matter. This is why we can SEE asteroids without touching them.
28–42 min

• Mini-lecture preview: Unit 6's payoffs. (1) Day 15 — RADAR and the Doppler shift that measured 2026-XJ's velocity. (2) Day 16 — the atmospheric glow as thermal-blackbody light (Wien's law). (3) Day 17 — seismic + tsunami waves carrying impact damage thousands of km.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "You wave the end of a rope. A pulse travels down the rope. What's moving through the rope: rope material, energy, or both?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1 preview.

Prep: Unit 5 transfer tasks scored; slinky (transverse-capable, like a long jump rope; not a slinky-style spring); a long rope as backup; whiteboard space.

Talk moves on "what's moving?": resist explaining. Push: "Did the END of the rope move along the rope? Did the energy move along the rope? Which one is the wave?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing the energy moving but the rope coil staying in place; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "how does ocean wave water move when a wave passes? Does the water travel with the wave?" (No — water moves in small circles. The wave's energy moves.)

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Wave properties: frequency, wavelength, amplitude, period
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — The vocabulary of waves

f, λ, A, T. Four numbers describe any wave.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Define wave properties.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Radar uses radio waves with f ≈ 10⁹ Hz (gigahertz). The shorter the wavelength, the smaller the object you can resolve. Today the vocabulary; Day 15 the application.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hand back exit tickets. Anchor: "To talk about waves we need vocabulary."
5–18 min

• Direct instruction with diagram on the board. Wavelength (λ) — distance from crest to crest, meters. Amplitude (A) — maximum displacement from equilibrium, meters. Period (T) — time for one full cycle, seconds. Frequency (f) — cycles per second, hertz (Hz). f and T are reciprocals: f = 1/T.
18–32 min

• Worked examples: (1) a wave has λ = 2 m and T = 0.5 s. Frequency? (2) a tuning fork at 440 Hz — period? (3) ocean wave with λ = 30 m, T = 6 s — frequency?
32–42 min

• PhET Waves on a String sim: vary f and A. See λ and A directly on the wave. Verify period = 1/f visually.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A wave has f = 250 Hz. What's the period?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1.

Prep: PhET Waves on a String loaded; wave-properties labeled diagram for the board.

Talk moves on f vs. T: when students confuse them, push: "How many cycles in one second is the FREQUENCY. How many seconds for one cycle is the PERIOD. They're reciprocals."

Differentiation: ELs — single labeled wave diagram with vocab terms attached; SWDs — pre-printed conversion (T → f and vice versa) practice; advanced — "a radio station broadcasts at 98.5 MHz. What's its wavelength? (Use v = c = 3×10⁸ m/s, preview Day 3.)"

title: Day 3 · Phase 1 — Wave speed: v = fλ
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 1 — The wave equation that ties it all together

v = fλ. Speed of a wave = frequency × wavelength.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. v = fλ. S1. Compute wave properties.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Radar waves travel at c = 3×10⁸ m/s. The time delay between sent and received pulse tells distance: d = (c × Δt)/2. Today's equation IS the radar equation.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: f, λ, T, A definitions.
5–15 min

• Direct instruction: v = fλ. Walk through where it comes from — in one period T, the wave moves a distance λ. Speed = λ/T = λ × f.
15–28 min

• Worked examples: (1) sound wave at 880 Hz with λ = 0.39 m — speed? (Get 343 m/s, the speed of sound.) (2) radio wave at 100 MHz — λ if v = c? (3 m.) (3) ocean wave at 30 m wavelength, traveling 5 m/s — frequency?
28–40 min

• Distinction: speed depends on MEDIUM, not on the source. A 440 Hz tuning fork makes 440 Hz sound in air OR water OR a steel rod — but the WAVELENGTH differs because v differs in each medium.
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "An FM radio wave has f = 95 MHz. What's its wavelength? (Use c = 3×10⁸ m/s.)"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K3/S1.

Prep: medium-speed reference card (sound in air ≈ 343, in water ≈ 1480, in steel ≈ 5960; light in vacuum = 3×10⁸); packet practice; calculator.

Talk moves on "frequency unchanged across media": push: "If the source vibrates 440 times per second, then 440 wavefronts leave it per second — no matter the medium. So WHAT changes when the wave enters water?"

Differentiation: ELs — v = fλ rearranged into all three forms; SWDs — speed-of-medium reference card; advanced — "if sound moves from air to water, does the WAVELENGTH increase or decrease? Why?"

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Reflection + transmission at boundaries
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — What happens at a boundary

Some bounces back. Some passes through. Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Reflection law. Reason about boundary behavior.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Radar works because radio waves REFLECT off the asteroid surface. Today is the foundation: what conditions does reflection require, what fraction reflects vs. transmits, and how does angle work.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick recap: v = fλ.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (measured from the normal). All waves obey this — sound off walls, light off mirrors, radio off asteroids, ocean waves off cliffs.
18–32 min

• Demos: (1) ray box + plane mirror — show angle equality. (2) wave tank or PhET Wave Interference — wave hitting boundary, see reflection. (3) sound echo if room geometry allows.
32–42 min

• Transmission: when a wave hits a boundary, SOME of it reflects, SOME transmits into the new medium. The ratio depends on the impedance contrast — a wave hits a brick wall, almost all reflects; hits a curtain, much passes through.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "You shine a laser at a mirror at 35° from the normal. At what angle does it reflect?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K4.

Prep: ray box + plane mirror; PhET Wave Interference (or wave tank); protractors.

Talk moves on "measured from normal not surface": when students measure from the surface, stop. "The convention is from the NORMAL — the line perpendicular to the surface. Where is the normal here?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing the normal line + incident + reflected angles; SWDs — pre-built worksheet with normal pre-drawn; advanced — "why does the law of reflection follow from energy conservation + momentum?"

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Refraction: bending at a boundary
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Speed change → direction change

When a wave enters a slower medium, it bends toward the normal. Snell's law (qualitative for CPA).

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Refraction. Reason about which way a wave bends.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Radar waves don't refract significantly in vacuum — but they DO when entering Earth's atmosphere. Sound from impact refracts in air (cooler air is denser → sound bends toward cooler regions). Refraction explains why we hear distant thunder.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: a pencil in a glass of water appears bent. Why?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: when a wave enters a NEW medium, speed changes → direction changes. Slower medium → bends TOWARD normal. Faster medium → bends AWAY. Show with a ray-box + glass block.
18–32 min

• Demo: marching-band analogy. Soldiers in a row hit mud at an angle — the side that hits mud first slows; the row twists. Same with waves entering water (slower).
32–42 min

• PhET Bending Light: vary the second-medium index. Watch the refraction angle change. Snell's law qualitatively: more bending if speeds are more different.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Light enters water from air. Does it bend toward or away from the normal? Why?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K4.

Prep: ray box + glass or acrylic block; PhET Bending Light; protractors.

Talk moves on "toward or away": when students guess, push: "Which medium does light move FASTER in? When it enters the slower one, what side of the wave slows first?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual with arrows for air-to-water case; SWDs — practice card with pre-marked normal; advanced — "why do mirages form on hot pavement? Hint: refraction in a temperature gradient."

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Diffraction: waves bending around obstacles
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — Bending around edges

Waves diffract around obstacles or through openings. More diffraction when λ is comparable to obstacle size.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Diffraction qualitatively.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Long-wavelength radar can see around small obstacles; short-wavelength can resolve details. Wavelength choice is the key engineering decision in radar design.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: you can HEAR someone around a corner but you can't SEE them. Why?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: waves don't always travel in straight lines. They diffract (bend) around obstacles or through openings. The smaller the obstacle relative to λ, the MORE diffraction. Sound (λ ≈ 1 m) diffracts around doorways easily. Light (λ ≈ 500 nm) barely diffracts around everyday objects.
18–30 min

• Demo: PhET Wave Interference in slit mode. Send a wave through a wide opening (no diffraction). Then a narrow opening (significant diffraction).
30–42 min

• Application: radar wavelength choice. Long radio waves (λ ~ m) diffract around mountains — used for over-the-horizon radar. Short waves (λ ~ cm) don't diffract around small objects — better for resolution.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why can you hear a conversation around a corner but not see the speaker?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K4.

Prep: PhET Wave Interference; doorway image or photo for context.

Talk moves on hear-vs-see: push: "What's the wavelength of sound? Of visible light? Why does that ratio matter?"

Differentiation: ELs — narrow-slit-vs-wide-slit diagram with diffraction shown; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "why can radio telescopes see giant features in galaxies that optical telescopes can't?"

title: Day 7 · Phase 2 — Interference: waves adding + canceling
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 2 — Constructive + destructive interference

Two waves meet. Crests + crests = bigger crests. Crests + troughs = NOTHING.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K5. Interference qualitatively. Standing waves as interference.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Modern radar uses INTERFEROMETRY — combining multiple radar signals to resolve fine asteroid details. Resonance is also how musical instruments work; preview Day 9.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: when waves meet at a boundary they reflect. What if they meet IN THE OPEN?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: when waves MEET, their displacements ADD. Constructive interference: crests align with crests → bigger wave. Destructive interference: crest meets trough → cancellation.
18–30 min

• Demo: PhET Wave Interference with two sources. Watch the interference pattern of bright + dark fringes (or loud + quiet zones for sound). Connect to noise-canceling headphones (destructive interference of sound).
30–42 min

• Standing waves: a special case of interference — wave reflected back interferes with itself. Creates fixed nodes (no motion) and antinodes (max motion). Demo with a slinky pulse reflected. This is how guitar strings + organ pipes work.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Noise-canceling headphones produce a sound wave OPPOSITE to incoming noise. Why does this cancel?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K5.

Prep: PhET Wave Interference; slinky; guitar (or musical instrument prop).

Talk moves on "opposite-wave cancels": when students struggle to articulate, push: "If two waves add and one has +5 displacement while the other has -5, what's the net? Where did the energy go?" (Spread to other places, or back into source — preserved.)

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing crest+crest and crest+trough addition; SWDs — pre-built worksheet with sample wave additions; advanced — "why does a guitar's specific length determine its lowest frequency?"

title: Day 8 · Phase 3 — Sound: longitudinal wave, pitch + amplitude
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 3 — Sound is compression waves

Higher f = higher pitch. Bigger A = louder. Sound needs a medium.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K6. Sound as a longitudinal mechanical wave. Pitch and loudness as wave properties.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An asteroid impact generates a LOUD sound — atmospheric shockwave, infrasound at very low frequencies that travels thousands of km. Today's foundation; Day 17 applies it.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: tap a tuning fork. What's WIGGLING to make the sound?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: sound is a longitudinal wave — compressions and rarefactions in a medium (air, water, solid). Frequency = pitch. Amplitude = loudness. Demo: 256 Hz tuning fork vs. 512 Hz — pitch difference. Same fork hit hard vs. soft — amplitude difference.
18–32 min

• Sound needs a medium — no air, no sound. Demo or video: bell jar evacuated, bell can't be heard. Why does space have no sound? Because there's no air. This is why explosions in space movies are silent — physically.
32–42 min

• Human hearing range: 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Infrasound (below 20) and ultrasound (above 20k) exist but we can't hear them. Asteroid impacts produce powerful INFRASOUND.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why is there no sound in outer space, even from a violent event?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K6.

Prep: tuning forks (256 + 512 Hz, or pairs); bell-jar demo or video; frequency-vs-pitch reference.

Talk moves on "no medium = no sound": push: "Sound is a wave IN a medium. What medium does space have between objects? Almost nothing. So sound can't propagate."

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing compression-rarefaction pattern; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "why can you sometimes hear an explosion before you see the smoke wave arrive? (Hint: speed of sound vs. speed of light.)"

title: Day 9 · Phase 3 — Speed of sound + resonance
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 3 — Speed of sound + standing waves in instruments

v_sound depends on medium. Instruments use standing waves at characteristic frequencies.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K6. Speed of sound in different media + resonance.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Today's resonance is the bridge to seismic waves (Day 17) — Earth has its own "ringing" frequencies that get excited by a large impact.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: v = fλ.
5–15 min

• Direct instruction: speed of sound in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C. Faster in water (~1480 m/s). Faster still in solids (~5000+ m/s in steel). Why? Stiffer medium → faster.
15–28 min

• Worked examples: sound's λ in air at 440 Hz. Same f in water — different λ. Echo timing — count to 3 seconds = sound traveled ~1 km.
28–42 min

• Resonance + standing waves: guitar string of length L resonates at f = v/(2L) — only certain frequencies fit. Pluck it and HEAR the fundamental + harmonics. Same for an organ pipe.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A guitar string is 65 cm long. The wave speed in the string is 80 m/s. What's the fundamental frequency?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K6.

Prep: guitar or stringed instrument; speed-of-sound reference; resonance demo (PhET or oscilloscope display of harmonics).

Talk moves on "why only certain frequencies": push: "For a standing wave on a fixed-end string, what's the longest wavelength that fits? Next longest? Why?"

Differentiation: ELs — pre-drawn standing-wave diagrams (fundamental, 2nd harmonic, 3rd); SWDs — fill-in worksheet (L, λ, f for each harmonic); advanced — "derive the harmonic series f_n = nv/(2L) for a string fixed at both ends."

title: Day 10 · Phase 3 — Vernier sound lab: measuring v_sound
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 3 — Empirical wave speed

Microphone + LabQuest. Measure speed of sound by timing echoes.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S4. Use Vernier microphone for wave measurement. K6. Confirm v_sound experimentally.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If we trust our v = fλ relationship in air, we can apply it to radar waves in vacuum (Day 15) and atmospheric sound from impact (Day 17).

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Lab procedure overview + safety beat.
5–15 min

• Method 1 demo: clap near a wall. Vernier microphone records the clap + its echo. Time between them × v = 2L (round trip). Compute v from a known L.
15–35 min

• Lab: pairs run 3 trials with known distance to wall. Capture clap waveform, find Δt to echo, compute v_sound. Average across groups. Compare to 343 m/s.
35–43 min

• Class data compilation. Discuss error sources: precise distance, peak-finding in waveform, room temperature (sound speed varies with T).
43–48 min

• Lab write-up start.
48–50 min

• Marzano on S4.

Prep: Vernier microphones + LabQuest; clear distance to wall (corridor or large room); tape measure; sample-rate set high enough; lab write-up template.

Talk moves on "why % differences are usually 1-2%": push: "What's hardest to measure precisely — the DISTANCE or the TIME? Which contributes more error?"

Differentiation: ELs — pair with LoggerPro-fluent student; SWDs — pre-built data table; advanced — "use frequency analysis to identify the dominant frequency of your clap. Why does its λ matter for echo crispness?"

title: Day 11 · Phase 3 — Intensity, decibels, Doppler effect
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 3 — Loudness measurement + frequency shift

I in W/m². dB is logarithmic. Doppler shifts frequency for moving sources/observers.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K7. Sound intensity + decibels. K8. Doppler effect. S2. Doppler shift direction.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Doppler is HOW radar measures asteroid velocity. A receding asteroid returns radar at LOWER frequency; approaching, HIGHER. The shift gives velocity directly. Day 15 is the payoff.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: amplitude vs. loudness.
5–18 min

• Intensity I = power/area, W/m². Inverse-square: I ∝ 1/r². Decibels: dB = 10·log₁₀(I/I_0), I_0 = 10⁻¹² W/m². Every +10 dB = ×10 in intensity. 0 dB hearing threshold, 60 dB normal conversation, 120 dB pain.
18–30 min

• Doppler effect: ambulance siren — higher pitch as it approaches, lower as it recedes. Why? Each successive wavefront emitted from a moving-toward observer is closer to the previous → shorter wavelength → higher frequency. Movement AWAY → longer λ → lower f.
30–42 min

• Worked examples: (1) ambulance at 800 Hz approaching at 30 m/s. Heard frequency? (Use f' = f × v_sound/(v_sound − v_source).) (2) star moving away from Earth at 100 km/s. Light frequency shifted — red shift. Distance to galaxy from redshift.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A train at 600 Hz horn moves at 20 m/s. You stand on the platform as it approaches. What frequency do you hear?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K7/K8/S2.

Prep: decibel reference chart; Doppler animation (PhET Wave Source); packet practice; calculator.

Talk moves on dB scale: when students treat it linearly, push: "60 dB is NOT twice as loud as 30 dB. What IS twice as loud? Why?"

Differentiation: ELs — dB reference chart with everyday examples; SWDs — pre-built Doppler equation with each variable labeled; advanced — "a galaxy emits H-alpha at 656 nm. We observe it at 700 nm. How fast is it receding?"

title: Day 12 · Phase 4 — Electromagnetic spectrum + light as a wave
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 4 — Light is an EM wave

EM spectrum: radio → microwave → IR → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma. All travel at c in vacuum.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K9. EM spectrum + light as electromagnetic wave.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Light from a glowing meteor (atmospheric entry) is the same KIND of wave as the radar we used to detect 2026-XJ. Different frequency, same family. Day 16 builds on this.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "What's the difference between radar (Day 1) and visible light? Both travel at c. Both are waves. What changes?"
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: electromagnetic spectrum. Walk through the bands by wavelength + frequency. Radio (km) → microwave (cm) → infrared (μm) → visible (400-700 nm) → UV (100 nm) → X-ray (nm) → gamma (sub-nm). Same wave, different f, different λ. Same speed c in vacuum.
18–32 min

• Visible light: ROYGBIV by decreasing λ (red ≈700 nm, violet ≈400 nm). What sets the color of an object? It REFLECTS certain wavelengths and absorbs others.
32–42 min

• Applications: heat (IR), radio (long-distance comm), X-ray (medical imaging — short λ goes through tissue but not bone). Each band has a use.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "What's the wavelength of a microwave oven's 2.4 GHz radiation? (Use c = 3×10⁸.) Which EM band is this?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K9.

Prep: EM-spectrum chart for the board; ROYGBIV reference; calculator availability.

Talk moves on "all the same wave": push: "If radio and light are the same wave family with different λ, why can't we SEE radio? What's special about visible?" (Our retinas evolved to detect the band the Sun emits most.)

Differentiation: ELs — EM spectrum visual with everyday-example annotations; SWDs — wavelength-to-band lookup card; advanced — "why is x-ray imaging dangerous but visible-light imaging safe?" (Photon energy ∝ frequency.)

title: Day 13 · Phase 4 — Light reflection + plane mirrors
bodyHtml:
DAY 13 · Phase 4 — Image formation in plane mirrors

Same reflection law as Day 4 — applied to light. Virtual images behind the mirror, distance = object distance.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K10. Light reflection + image formation in plane mirrors.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Radar telescopes are GIANT mirrors that focus radio waves to a detector. The geometry is the same as light mirrors. Today's foundation; Day 15 application.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Day 4 reflection law.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: when you look in a mirror, light from your face reflects off the mirror and into your eye. Your brain TRACES that light back in a straight line — it appears to come from BEHIND the mirror. That's a virtual image.
18–32 min

• Ray diagrams: pick two rays from object, follow reflection, trace back. Image is upright, same size, equal distance behind mirror. Demo with mirror + ruler.
32–42 min

• Practice ray-tracing: 4 problems with mirror and object at different positions. Locate image with construction.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 2-m-tall person stands 1.5 m from a mirror. Where is their image? How tall does it appear?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K10.

Prep: ray box + plane mirror; ruler; ray-tracing worksheet; protractor.

Talk moves on "virtual image": when students think the image is real and in the mirror, stop. "Could you put a screen at the image location and CATCH the image? Where would the screen go?" (Behind the mirror — but the light doesn't actually go there. That's why it's virtual.)

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing two rays from object, reflection, and traced-back image; SWDs — pre-printed ray diagram template; advanced — "why are PARABOLIC mirrors used in telescopes instead of flat ones?" (Focusing — preview Day 14's lens reasoning.)

title: Day 14 · Phase 4 — Light refraction + lenses (qualitative)
bodyHtml:
DAY 14 · Phase 4 — Light bending through lenses

Convex lens converges; concave diverges. Real vs. virtual images.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K10. Lens action qualitatively. Distinguish convex vs. concave.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Telescope eyepieces are lenses; objective mirrors collect; lenses focus. Modern asteroid optical telescopes combine both. Today's qualitative foundation supports Day 15's radar payoff.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Day 5 refraction — wave bends at boundary.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: a convex lens (thicker in middle) CONVERGES parallel rays to a focal point. A concave lens (thinner in middle) DIVERGES them. Each lens has a focal length f.
18–30 min

• Image formation: object beyond f from convex lens → real, inverted image. Object within f → virtual, magnified, upright (magnifying glass). Demo with a convex lens + object + screen.
30–42 min

• Applications: camera, eye, telescope, microscope, projector. Different combinations of lens + distance = different uses.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why does looking through a magnifying glass make small print BIGGER? Why does it ALSO appear closer than the print actually is?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K10.

Prep: convex + concave lens demos; bright object (candle or light bulb) + screen for image formation; ray-tracing worksheet.

Talk moves on "real vs. virtual": push: "Can you catch a real image on a screen? Can you catch a virtual one?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual of ray diagrams for convex + concave; SWDs — pre-drawn lens diagrams with focal point marked; advanced — "why does a corrective lens for myopia (nearsightedness) use a concave lens?"

title: Day 15 · Phase 5 — Radar: how we measured 2026-XJ's velocity
bodyHtml:
DAY 15 · Phase 5 — The detection payoff

Doppler + reflection + EM waves all combine to give us the asteroid's velocity vector.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R4. Apply EM wave framework to radar measurement.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The reveal day: every NUMBER we've used about 2026-XJ traces to a Doppler-shifted radar return. Today we trace the full chain.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame: "Today is the unit's reveal. Walk through how NASA actually measured 2026-XJ's velocity."
5–18 min

• Step 1 — DISTANCE. Send radar pulse. Wait for return. d = c × Δt/2. Worked example: round-trip time of 2.5 s gives d = 375,000 km. Within a few %.
18–32 min

• Step 2 — VELOCITY. Doppler shift. Transmitted 2.4 GHz. Received 2.40005 GHz (5 kHz higher). That's a velocity TOWARD Earth: v = (Δf/f) × c. Worked example: 5 kHz at 2.4 GHz returns v ≈ 312 m/s closing. Real asteroid measurements are far more precise.
32–42 min

• Step 3 — SIZE estimate. Signal strength vs. expected gives a radar cross-section, which combined with assumed albedo gives a rough size estimate. Mass needs an assumed density. (Why mass numbers have UNCERTAINTY.)
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why does NASA need MULTIPLE radar observations of an asteroid to predict its orbit precisely?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R4.

Prep: chalkboard diagram of radar pulse + return; calculator; the 2026-XJ data card from Unit 1 (radar return numbers).

Talk moves on uncertainty: push: "How much could the mass be off if our density assumption is wrong by 30%? How does that affect impact KE?"

Differentiation: ELs — chain-diagram of radar steps; SWDs — pre-built distance + velocity computation worksheet; advanced — "why is a long observing baseline (multiple weeks of data) crucial for orbit precision?"

title: Day 16 · Phase 5 — Atmospheric entry light: what color does the meteor glow?
bodyHtml:
DAY 16 · Phase 5 — Blackbody radiation + Wien's law

Hot objects emit light. Peak wavelength shifts with temperature. T determines color.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R4. Reason about thermal radiation color (qualitative Wien's law).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Atmospheric entry heat is THOUSANDS of kelvin. The meteor glows YELLOW-WHITE, sometimes BLUE-WHITE for the fastest entries. Today's content explains why.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: an electric stove burner at low heat glows red. At high heat — orange or yellow. Why does color depend on temperature?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: ALL warm objects emit EM radiation (thermal radiation). The hotter, the higher the FREQUENCY of peak emission. Wien's law (qualitative): peak λ shifts toward shorter wavelengths as T rises. Cool stars (3000 K) glow RED. Sun (6000 K) glows YELLOW-WHITE. Hottest stars (30000 K) glow BLUE.
18–30 min

• Meteor entry: surface heats to 5000-10000 K via atmospheric drag. Peak emission is yellow-white, with significant UV. The bright trail you see is thermal radiation, not flame.
30–42 min

• Application: astronomers measure star color to estimate surface temperature. The same logic identifies impact debris by glow color.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A meteor entering at extreme speed could heat to 10000 K. What color would it glow? How does this compare to a typical meteor (5000 K)?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R4.

Prep: Wien's-law color reference chart; stove or candle-flame image; calculator availability.

Talk moves on "why does the color shift with T?": push: "What property of the WAVE corresponds to its energy? (Higher f, more energy.) Why would hotter objects emit higher-energy waves?"

Differentiation: ELs — color-temperature visual showing each band; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "compute T given a peak λ using Wien's law constant: T·λ_max = 2.9×10⁻³ m·K. For peak 580 nm, what T?"

title: Day 17 · Phase 5 — Seismic + tsunami: mechanical wave damage at distance
bodyHtml:
DAY 17 · Phase 5 — Impact damage that travels far

Seismic waves through Earth's crust. Tsunami waves on ocean surface. Both can damage thousands of km from impact.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R4. Reason about wave-borne impact damage at distance.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If 2026-XJ hits the ocean, a tsunami can reach distant coastlines hours later. If it hits land, seismic waves register on the other side of the planet within minutes. The unit closes by modeling these waves.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame: "Energy at the impact point becomes mechanical waves that travel far."
5–18 min

• Seismic waves: P-waves (longitudinal, 5-6 km/s in mantle), S-waves (transverse, 3-4 km/s). Earthquake-style waves from impact. Compute time for P-waves to travel across Earth (diameter ≈ 12700 km / 6 km/s ≈ 35 min).
18–32 min

• Tsunami waves: ocean-surface gravity wave. Speed in deep water v ≈ √(g·d), where d is ocean depth. In a 4-km-deep ocean, v ≈ 200 m/s ≈ 700 km/h. Wave height grows dramatically as it approaches shallow coastline.
32–42 min

• Worked example: 2026-XJ ocean impact at 4-km depth. Tsunami reaches coast 1500 km away in: time = 1500/200 ≈ 7500 s ≈ 2 hours. The DAMAGE TIME for distant coasts.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why is a tsunami much more dangerous on the shoreline than in deep ocean?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R4.

Prep: seismic wave reference (P + S types and speeds); tsunami wave-speed equation; calculator availability.

Talk moves on tsunami amplification: push: "In deep water, the wave is fast but low amplitude. Why does it slow AND amplify when it reaches shallow water?" (Energy conservation — wave can't travel as fast, but the energy stays. Slower wave with same energy = taller.)

Differentiation: ELs — wave-speed-in-medium visual; SWDs — pre-built time-to-coast calculation; advanced — "estimate the seismic magnitude (Richter scale) of 2026-XJ's impact, given its impact KE."

title: Day 18 · Phase 6 — Unit 6 transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 18 · Phase 6 — Assessment of learning

5 multi-part problems integrating wave properties, behavior, sound, EM spectrum, and asteroid waves.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 6 targets, surfaced through transfer problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The transfer task includes a radar-derives-velocity problem (R4) and an impact-tsunami-arrival problem (R4) as the load-bearing Reasoning prompts.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Brief reminder of the 4-dimension rubric (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer). Lowest-dimension grading.
5–45 min

• Transfer task. Independent. 5 multi-part problems: (1) v = fλ across media (sound in air → water); (2) reflection + image location in a plane mirror; (3) Doppler shift for a moving sound source; (4) EM spectrum — given a frequency, identify band + compute wavelength; (5) asteroid scenario — given radar return time + Doppler shift, compute distance and velocity, then predict tsunami arrival time on a coast.
45–48 min

• Final Marzano on the unit target list.
48–50 min

• Growth chart updated. Unit 7 begins tomorrow — electricity + magnetism brings us to the foundations the Car Project depends on.

Prep: transfer task printed; calculators; constants reference card (c, v_sound, dB equation, Doppler); quiet testing environment; growth chart pages.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — vocabulary glossary attached (frequency, wavelength, reflection, refraction, Doppler, spectrum); SWDs — option to verbal-explain dimension 4 (Transfer) of any problem; advanced — bonus prompt: "Use Unit 6 thinking to estimate the EARLIEST detection time for a 2026-XJ-sized asteroid given current radar capabilities."

Closing: Growth chart picked up tomorrow at start of Unit 7 — electricity + magnetism, the foundation for the Car Project capstone.
